simulation.title: ScaleTrip Cloud — Backend Platform Engineering
simulation.description: Join ScaleTrip Cloud's Backend Platform Engineering team and stabilise a production SaaS platform serving 1.2 million booking events per month before a hard 1 July 2026 deadline. Fix a duplicate booking bug, optimise slow PostgreSQL queries, harden JWT authentication, implement BullMQ queue deduplication, repair broken CI/CD pipelines, and deploy to Kubernetes — using a real GitHub repository with intentional production-grade bugs.

company.name: ScaleTrip Cloud
company.about: Cloud-native booking infrastructure processing 1.2 million booking events monthly for travel agencies, hotel operators, and airline consolidators across Europe and North America.

inbox_messages.subject: Welcome to ScaleTrip — we need you up to speed fast
inbox_messages.body:
Welcome to the Backend Platform Engineering team.

I'm going to be direct with you: we're in a difficult position. The platform is processing 1.2 million booking events a month and we have 14 enterprise clients on contractual SLAs. The summer travel surge starts 1 July. That gives us less than 30 days.

Marcus is leaving on 20 June. His handover notes are in docs/process_notes.md — read them carefully, but be aware they're incomplete.

Your first task is to get across the full system state. Clone the repo, read the incident reports in incidents/, look at the data files in data/, and produce an architecture risk analysis and incident prioritisation. I need to know you understand what we're dealing with.

The duplicate booking issue (INC-442) is the most critical problem. 3,842 duplicates in Q1 alone. The Travelport account manager has already escalated.

Repo: https://github.com/alain-Sortnext/scaletrip-platform

Let's talk end of week.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Phase 2 — TypeScript migration is blocking everything
inbox_messages.body:
Now that you understand the system state, we need to start fixing things.

First priority: the TypeScript migration. booking-service/src/routes/bookings.js is still JavaScript. The type errors in shared/types/Booking.ts are causing tsc to fail with --skipLibCheck removed, which means our build pipeline is broken.

I need you to:
1. Fix the TypeScript error in shared/types/Booking.ts line 42
2. Add Zod validation to the booking creation endpoint
3. Run tsc --noEmit and confirm zero errors
4. Fix the ESLint config — that 'scaletrip-custom' plugin reference is what's breaking CI

The CI pipeline has been broken for 11 days. Every deployment is manual. That has to end.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Re: DB optimisation — good work, now auth
inbox_messages.body:
I reviewed your database analysis. The EXPLAIN ANALYZE output confirms the sequential scan — that index is exactly what we need.

Before you merge the migration, test it on a copy of the production dataset. The bookings table has 280,000 rows in production — run the EXPLAIN ANALYZE there too.

Next: auth service. Fatima has flagged a compliance issue I need you to look at before anything else. She'll send you a message directly. Do not proceed with the auth refactor until you've read her note.

The JWT logging issue she's identified is a potential UK GDPR Article 33 reportable incident. We cannot go live until it's fixed.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Phase 5 — this is the gate. Fix the queue or we don't ship.
inbox_messages.body:
This phase is the certification gate. Until the duplicate booking bug is resolved with verified evidence, I cannot sign off on the platform as production-ready.

Marcus left a clue in the process notes — look at the lock_acquired column in data/queue_metrics.csv. The pattern there tells you exactly what's happening.

I need from you:
1. A Redis distributed lock implementation in bookingWorker.ts
2. Dead-letter queue configuration
3. A root cause analysis with the specific figures from the data files
4. Evidence that the fix works — queue logs showing lock_acquired = true

The Travelport account goes live 2 July. If INC-442 happens again, we lose the contract.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Re: Queue fix — verified. Moving to infrastructure.
inbox_messages.body:
I reviewed your root cause analysis and the queue implementation evidence.

Your identification of the Redis lock gap and the 18.7% duplicate rate figures are correct. The lock_acquired_false spike in weeks 9-11 is exactly the signal we needed someone to find and act on.

Callum will brief you on the Kubernetes situation next. The staging pods are crashlooping — he suspects an env var mismatch. Get that fixed and then we can run a proper load test before go-live.

Good work on this phase. This is the kind of engineering rigour we need.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Final review — you're almost there
inbox_messages.body:
You've come a long way from the chaos you inherited.

For your final phase, I need three things:
1. A verbal engineering walkthrough — 90 seconds covering the biggest issue you fixed, the root cause, and the operational impact. I'll use this in the Travelport account review.
2. Architecture Decision Records for the queue architecture and deployment strategy.
3. A production readiness sign-off that I can share with the board.

The ICO pre-audit is 15 July. Everything Fatima flagged must be resolved and documented before then.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Handover — everything I know (which isn't everything)
inbox_messages.body:
Hello and welcome.

I'm Marcus. I'm leaving on 20 June — not ideal timing I know.

I've written up what I can in docs/process_notes.md. The honest answer is it's not complete. Notification-service, the AWS SQS integration, and the Kubernetes configs are all Callum's domain and I've barely touched them.

The thing I feel worst about leaving: the duplicate booking bug. I know what it is. The bookingWorker.ts has no Redis distributed lock before the processBooking() call. Under high concurrency, two workers grab the same job and both write to the database.

Look at the queue_metrics.csv file. The lock_acquired column — when that's false during high-throughput windows, that's the bug in action.

I tested the fix locally. It's about 15 lines of code. I just ran out of time.

The test in duplicate.test.js is skipped with describe.skip — that needs to be unskipped and made to pass after you implement the lock.

Good luck. The team is solid. The platform just needs proper engineering attention.

Marcus

inbox_messages.subject: One more thing — the TypeScript error
inbox_messages.body:
I just remembered — the TypeScript error on line 42 of shared/types/Booking.ts.

The BookingQueryParams interface has tenantId typed as string, but it comes from req.query which TypeScript correctly infers as string | undefined. Change it to string | undefined and the compile error disappears.

Also: the .eslintrc.json references a 'scaletrip-custom' plugin that doesn't exist. I added it as a placeholder for a custom rule we were going to write but never did. Just remove it from the plugins array and the lint stage will pass.

Those two fixes together should get CI green.

Marcus

inbox_messages.subject: API performance data — it's not what it looks like
inbox_messages.body:
I've seen your architecture analysis. You're right that the query latency is the most visible symptom.

I pulled the raw metrics — they're in data/api_performance_metrics.csv. Look at week 10. The p95 is 4,247ms.

Here's the thing: request volume went up in those weeks too, so it looks like a traffic problem. It isn't. Look at the db_conn_pool_exhausted column. When that's true, it means the query is holding database connections open while it scans every row for the tenant. The pool hits its limit and new requests queue.

The slow_query_log column tells you exactly which query. One composite index on (tenant_id, created_at) fixes this. I've seen it drop from 4,200ms to under 200ms in similar setups.

Also: add LIMIT/OFFSET pagination. That endpoint returns all rows. At production scale that's 280,000 rows per query.

Callum

inbox_messages.subject: Kubernetes — I think I know why they're crashlooping
inbox_messages.body:
The booking-service pods have been crashlooping in staging for two weeks.

I've been managing it manually — too much on my plate with the Travelport migration.

My suspicion: look at infrastructure/kubernetes/booking-deployment.yaml. The env section. The service code expects DATABASE_URL but I think the K8s config is passing DB_URL. That would cause the service to start, fail to connect, crash, restart, fail, crash — CrashLoopBackOff.

Run: kubectl describe pod <booking-pod-name> and look at the last few lines of the log. It'll tell you the exact error.

Also: there are no liveness or readiness probes configured. Once you fix the env var and the pod starts successfully, add the probes — the /health endpoint already exists in booking-service, but /ready doesn't. You'll need to add that endpoint too.

I'm in the Travelport migration all week but ping me if you get stuck on the K8s config.

Callum

inbox_messages.subject: URGENT — potential UK GDPR Article 33 issue in auth-service
inbox_messages.body:
I need to flag this before you go any further with the auth refactor.

The auth-service logger (src/middleware/logger.ts) is logging the full Authorization header — including the Bearer token — to CloudWatch. Full JWT tokens in logs constitute personal data under the UK GDPR. If those logs were ever accessed without authorisation, we would have an Article 33 reportable breach.

This MUST be fixed before go-live. The fix is straightforward: strip the Authorization header from req.headers before passing to the logger.

While you're in there:
1. The booking endpoints accept tenant_id from the request body — this is an OWASP API1 Broken Object Level Authorisation vulnerability. tenant_id must come from the verified JWT claims only.
2. The CORS policy in gateway/src/index.ts is origin: '*'. This must be restricted to our known client origins.
3. The payment-service logs the full card_data object at DEBUG level. That's a PCI DSS violation — card data must never appear in logs.

I'm sending the ICO pre-audit checklist separately. We have until 15 July.

Fatima Al-Hassan
Head of Compliance & Security

tools.name: GitHub
tools.purpose: Clone the scaletrip-platform repo, make commits, open PRs — all work is tracked here.
signup_steps: Go to github.com/signup
Create a free account
Clone the repo: git clone https://github.com/alain-Sortnext/scaletrip-platform.git
cd scaletrip-platform
npm install
Verify: git log --oneline -5

tools.name: VS Code + Node.js LTS + TypeScript
tools.purpose: Write and compile TypeScript. Run tsc --noEmit. Execute npm scripts. Required for all code phases.
signup_steps: Install VS Code from code.visualstudio.com/download
Install Node.js LTS from nodejs.org
Open terminal: node --version (should be v20+)
cd scaletrip-platform && npm install
Install TS globally: npm install -g typescript
Run: npx tsc --noEmit to see current errors
Install VS Code extensions: ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript

tools.name: Docker Desktop + Docker Compose
tools.purpose: Run all services locally. Build production Dockerfiles. Replicate the deployment environment.
signup_steps: Download Docker Desktop from docker.com/products/docker-desktop
Install with default settings and restart your computer
Open terminal: docker --version (should show 27.x.x)
cd scaletrip-platform
docker-compose up -d
docker ps — verify postgres and redis containers are running
docker-compose logs booking-service — check for startup errors

tools.name: PostgreSQL + pgAdmin
tools.purpose: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries. Add indexes. Verify query performance before and after optimisation.
signup_steps: Docker Compose starts PostgreSQL automatically on port 5432
Download pgAdmin 4 from pgadmin.org/download
Open pgAdmin — click Add New Server
Host: localhost, Port: 5432, Username: postgres, Password: postgres
Connect to the scaletrip database
Open Query Tool (Tools menu)
Run: EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM bookings WHERE tenant_id = 'T-001' ORDER BY created_at DESC
Note the Planning Time, Execution Time, and whether it shows 'Seq Scan' or 'Index Scan'

tools.name: Postman (or Hoppscotch)
tools.purpose: Test authentication endpoints. Verify 401/403 responses. Test refresh token flow. Demonstrate BOLA fix.
signup_steps: Download Postman from postman.com/downloads OR use Hoppscotch at hoppscotch.io (browser, no install)
Create a new Collection named 'ScaleTrip Platform'
Add a POST request to http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login
Body (JSON): {"email": "admin@scaletrip.io", "password": "test"}
Send — copy the access_token from the response
Create a GET request to http://localhost:3000/api/bookings
Add header: Authorization: Bearer <your-token>
Send — note the response and whether tenant isolation works

tools.name: Minikube + Lens Desktop
tools.purpose: Deploy the platform to a local Kubernetes cluster. Debug crashlooping pods. Apply and test fixed YAML configs.
signup_steps: Install minikube from minikube.sigs.k8s.io/docs/start
minikube start --driver=docker
minikube status — should show: Running
kubectl get nodes — should show: minikube Ready
kubectl create namespace scaletrip
Download Lens from k8slens.dev (visual K8s dashboard)
Open Lens — connect to your minikube cluster
You'll see the crashlooping pods — your job is to fix them

tools.name: AWS Free Tier
tools.purpose: Configure S3 bucket, CloudWatch log groups, SQS queue, and IAM roles for the platform.
signup_steps: Go to aws.amazon.com/free and create a free account
Sign in to the AWS Console at console.aws.amazon.com
Set your region to eu-west-2 (London)
Services used: S3, CloudWatch, SQS, IAM — all in Free Tier
Install AWS CLI: brew install awscli OR download from aws.amazon.com/cli
aws configure — enter your Access Key ID and Secret from IAM console
aws sts get-caller-identity — verify your account

content.headline: Fix a crashing production SaaS platform before 40,000 summer bookings arrive
content.description: ScaleTrip Cloud processes 1.2 million booking events per month for travel agencies across Europe. Rapid growth broke the platform. You join the Backend Platform Engineering team 30 days before a hard go-live deadline — with a real GitHub repo, real broken code, and real production incidents to investigate.
content.employer_section_title: Why UK TravelTech and B2B SaaS companies value this simulation
content.employer_context: Backend Platform Engineers at Skyscanner, TravelPerk, and Amadeus spend their first months fixing exactly these problems: duplicate processing bugs, slow queries, broken CI pipelines, and Kubernetes crashloops. This simulation is built from real Q1 2026 postings at those companies.

content.about: Cloud-native booking infrastructure serving 1.2 million booking events per month for travel agencies, hotel operators, and airline consolidators across Europe and North America. Series B (£28M). 94 employees.
content.your_role: You are joining as a Backend Platform Engineer. Your deliverables: fix the duplicate booking bug (INC-442), optimise the slow GET /bookings query, harden JWT authentication, repair the broken CI pipeline, and containerise all services for Kubernetes deployment — all before 1 July 2026.
content.fun_fact: ScaleTrip processed 47,000 concurrent sessions in August 2025 — and the platform nearly fell over. You're here to make sure that doesn't happen again in August 2026.

content.intro_text: Four people you'll be working with throughout the simulation. Read their pressures carefully — each one will give you context that matters.

team_members.bio: Directly accountable to the CTO for the 1 July delivery. Running point on the Travelport account relationship. Has been shielding the team from commercial pressure but has no more runway. Needs evidence, not reassurance.
Leaving 20 June for Monzo. Built booking-service and the JWT layer. Knows exactly what's wrong with the queue but ran out of time. His handover is honest about what he didn't document.

Owns AWS, Kubernetes, and Terraform. Swamped with the Travelport migration. Has seen the API performance data and has a strong suspicion about the Kubernetes crashloop cause — but needs someone to actually fix it.
ICO pre-audit is 15 July. Has already identified three compliance gaps that must be resolved before go-live. Not optional. Not negotiable. Will escalate to the CTO if they're not addressed.

content.question: The api_performance_metrics.csv shows the p95 API latency at its worst point in Q1 2026. What was that figure?
options: 1,200ms — elevated but within SLA tolerance
2,800ms — concerning but manageable
4,247ms — well above the 500ms SLA threshold
8,900ms — gateway timeout territory
content.explanation: The p95 latency peaked at 4,247ms in Week 10 — nearly 9x the 500ms SLA target. This was caused by a sequential table scan on the bookings table. The fix is a composite index on (tenant_id, created_at).

deterministic_checks.failure_feedback: Incorrect. The p95 latency peaked at 4,247ms in Week 10 — nearly 9x the 500ms SLA target. This was caused by a sequential table scan on the bookings table. The fix is a composite index on (tenant_id, created_at).

sections.title: Quiz — Q2: The Duplicate Booking Bug

content.question: In which file does the duplicate booking bug live — the specific file that needs a Redis distributed lock?
options: services/booking-service/src/repository/BookingRepository.js
services/booking-service/src/routes/bookings.js
services/payment-service/src/webhooks/handler.ts
services/booking-service/src/queue/bookingWorker.ts
content.explanation: The bug is in bookingWorker.ts. The processBooking() call has no Redis distributed lock before it — so two concurrent workers can both process the same job and create duplicate bookings. BookingRepository.js has the slow query issue, not the duplicate bug.

deterministic_checks.failure_feedback: Incorrect. The bug is in bookingWorker.ts. The processBooking() call has no Redis distributed lock before it — so two concurrent workers can both process the same job and create duplicate bookings. BookingRepository.js has the slow query issue, not the duplicate bug.

content.question: Fatima Al-Hassan has identified a UK GDPR issue in auth-service. What is the violation?
options: The auth service is not encrypting passwords with bcrypt
The auth service logs the full Authorization: Bearer <token> header to CloudWatch
The auth service stores refresh tokens in plaintext in PostgreSQL
The auth service uses HS256 instead of RS256 for JWT signing
content.explanation: The auth-service logger.ts logs req.headers to CloudWatch — which includes the full Authorization: Bearer <jwt_token> header. Bearer tokens in logs are personal data under UK GDPR. If those logs were compromised, it would be a reportable breach under Article 33.

Bearer tokens in logs are personal data under UK GDPR. If those logs were compromised, it would be a reportable breach under Article 33.

content.question: What is the root cause of the GitHub Actions CI pipeline failure that has blocked deployments for 11 days?
options: Jest tests are failing due to an async timeout in auth.test.ts
The Docker build fails because the production stage is missing from the Dockerfile
The .eslintrc.json references an ESLint plugin called 'scaletrip-custom' that does not exist
The TypeScript compilation fails because tsc is not installed in the CI runner
content.explanation: The .eslintrc.json has 'scaletrip-custom' in the plugins array — a plugin that was planned but never created. Every lint run fails with 'Definition for rule not found.' Remove it from the plugins array and the lint stage passes. The TypeScript error in shared/types/Booking.ts is also real but secondary.

content.text:
## Phase 2 — TypeScript API Refactor

The booking-service is partially TypeScript and partially JavaScript. The shared type library has a compile error. The ESLint config is broken. Until you fix these, CI is blocked and every deployment is manual.

**Your first real commit to this platform fixes the foundation everything else depends on.**

Clone the repo if you haven't already:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/alain-Sortnext/scaletrip-platform.git
cd scaletrip-platform
```

hints.text:
The wrong-direction trap: traffic volume doubles in weeks 9-11. Most candidates blame traffic. The real cause is db_conn_pool_exhausted=true in the same weeks — the query holds connections open during the full table scan.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE (opening):

## Peak Latency
p95 peak: 4,247ms (Week 10, api_performance_metrics.csv)

## Root Cause
Not traffic volume. The db_conn_pool_exhausted column = true in weeks 9-11. The SELECT * query holds a connection open while scanning 280,000 rows.

The root cause analysis: look at the slow_query_log column in api_performance_metrics.csv for weeks 9-11. It shows 'SELECT * FROM bookings WHERE tenant_id=$1 ORDER BY created_at DESC (no index)'.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

## Root Cause
The slow_query_log column in api_performance_metrics.csv confirms: 'SELECT * FROM bookings WHERE tenant_id=$1 ORDER BY created_at DESC (no index)'. Without a composite index on (tenant_id, created_at), PostgreSQL does a sequential scan of all 280,000 rows for every request. Each scan holds a connection open for ~4 seconds, exhausting the 10-connection pool.

Complete expert report.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

## Peak Latency
p95: 4,247ms Week 10 — 8.5x above the 500ms SLA threshold.

## Root Cause
db_conn_pool_exhausted=true in weeks 9-11 (api_performance_metrics.csv). The SELECT * query holds each connection open for ~4 seconds during a full sequential scan. At peak load, all 10 connections are held, new requests queue, latency compounds. Callum's instinct about traffic was partially right — high traffic triggered it — but the index is the fix.

## Fix
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_bookings_tenant_created ON bookings (tenant_id, created_at DESC);
Before: 4,247ms (Seq Scan, 280,000 rows). After: <200ms (Index Scan). 21x improvement.

## Pagination
Added LIMIT 50 OFFSET $2 to BookingRepository.getBookingsByTenant(). Unbounded queries return all 280,000 rows — regardless of the index — saturating memory and network.

content.template:
## Security Analysis Report

### Issue 1 — JWT Logging (UK GDPR Article 33)

### Issue 2 — Cross-Tenant BOLA (OWASP API1)

### Issue 3 — CORS Misconfiguration (OWASP API8)

### Issue 4 — Missing Refresh Token Flow

### Remaining Risks

hints.text:
Structure each issue as: WHAT was wrong → WHY it's a risk → HOW you fixed it → REGULATION or OWASP reference.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE (Issue 1 structure):

### JWT Logging (UK GDPR Article 33)
WHAT: auth-service/src/middleware/logger.ts logs req.headers including Authorization: Bearer <token>.
RISK: Bearer tokens in logs = personal data. CloudWatch breach = Article 33 reportable.
FIX: Strip Authorization from headers before logging.
REGULATION: UK GDPR Article 33 — breach notification within 72 hours.

For the BOLA section: explain that the attack vector is an authenticated user from T-001 sending tenant_id='T-002' in the request body to read another tenant's bookings.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE (BOLA):

### BOLA (OWASP API1)
ATTACK: Authenticated user from T-001 sends GET /bookings?tenant_id=T-002. Current code reads tenant_id from query params — returns T-002's bookings.
FIX: const tenantId = req.user.tenantId — reads from verified JWT claims. User cannot override.
Impact: Prevents any tenant reading another tenant's booking data.

Complete security analysis.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

### JWT Logging — UK GDPR Article 33
logger.ts logged full req.headers including Authorization: Bearer <jwt>. JWT tokens contain userId and tenantId — personal data under UK GDPR. Fix: destructure headers and exclude authorization key before logging. Risk level: potential Article 33 reportable breach.

### BOLA — OWASP API1
tenant_id from req.body allowed cross-tenant access. Fixed by reading req.user.tenantId from verified JWT. Tested: T-001 token cannot access T-002 data.

### CORS — OWASP API8
cors({ origin: '*' }) allows any domain to make authenticated requests. Fixed: cors({ origin: ['https://app.scaletrip.io', 'https://staging.scaletrip.io'] }).

### Refresh Tokens
15-minute access token with no refresh = 14% session abandon. Implemented POST /auth/refresh: validates refresh token → issues new access token.

## Root Cause Analysis — INC-442

### Figures from Data

### Evidence

hints.text:
To calculate the duplicate rate: in a SQL client or spreadsheet, count rows where is_duplicate='true' and divide by total rows × 100. In booking_events.csv, 20 rows have is_duplicate='true' out of 107 total.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE (opening):

## Figures from Data
- Duplicate rate: 18.7% (20 of 107 rows in booking_events.csv have is_duplicate='true')
- DLQ growth (weeks 10-11): 12.3% average (queue_metrics.csv, dlq_count/total_jobs)

The lock_acquired_false column in queue_metrics.csv spikes in weeks 9-11 — the exact same weeks as the duplicate booking surge in booking_events.csv. This is the cross-reference that proves the root cause.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

## Evidence
queue_metrics.csv shows lock_acquired_false = 3,745 events in week 9, 2,621 in week 10, 2,730 in week 11. These weeks exactly match the 45%/45%/42% duplicate rates in booking_events.csv. This cross-reference proves the root cause: two workers acquiring the same BullMQ job simultaneously.

Complete root cause analysis.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

## Root Cause Analysis — INC-442

### Figures
- Duplicate rate: 18.7% (20/107 rows, booking_events.csv)
- DLQ growth (weeks 10-11): 12.3% average (queue_metrics.csv)
- Storm duration (14 March): 4.2 hours, retry_interval_ms fixed at 500ms

### Root Cause
No Redis distributed lock in bookingWorker.ts. BullMQ delivers jobs to 5 concurrent workers. Under high load, two workers dequeue the same job before BullMQ marks it as processing. Both call BookingService.create() — both succeed — creating a duplicate booking_ref.

### Evidence
lock_acquired_false spikes in weeks 9-11 (queue_metrics.csv) match exactly with the duplicate surge (booking_events.csv). The cross-reference is definitive.

### Fix
const lock = await redis.set(`lock:booking:${ref}`,'1',{NX:true,PX:30000});
if (!lock) throw new Error('DUPLICATE_JOB');

NX:true = set only if not exists. PX:30000 = 30s TTL prevents deadlock.

### Business Impact
£82,000 compensation exposure eliminated. 3,842 duplicates/quarter reduced to 0.

content.question: What is the technical root cause of the duplicate booking surge described in INC-442?
options: The booking_ref column in PostgreSQL lacked a UNIQUE constraint
A database transaction was missing a rollback handler
The bookingWorker.ts had no Redis distributed lock before calling processBooking() — two concurrent workers processed the same BullMQ job simultaneously
The payment-service was sending duplicate webhook events to the booking queue
content.explanation: The root cause is the missing Redis distributed lock in bookingWorker.ts. BullMQ does not guarantee at-most-once delivery under high concurrency without a distributed lock. Two workers dequeued the same job when the payment gateway introduced latency — both called processBooking() — both wrote to the database. A database UNIQUE constraint would have caught it at the DB layer but is not the root cause.

deterministic_checks.failure_feedback: Incorrect. The root cause is the missing Redis distributed lock in bookingWorker.ts. BullMQ does not guarantee at-most-once delivery under high concurrency without a distributed lock. Two workers dequeued the same job when the payment gateway introduced latency — both called processBooking() — both wrote to the database. A database UNIQUE constraint would have caught it at the DB layer but is not the root cause.

content.question: After adding a composite index on (tenant_id, created_at DESC) to the bookings table, what should the p95 latency of GET /bookings drop to in a well-tuned PostgreSQL system?
options: Under 200ms — from a sequential scan to an index scan on a bounded dataset
Under 1,000ms — a realistic improvement given the 280,000 row count
Under 2,000ms — indexes help but the dataset is too large for sub-second response
Under 500ms — the SLA threshold, which is achievable with the index alone
content.explanation: Under 200ms is the expected outcome. EXPLAIN ANALYZE after index creation should show 'Index Scan using idx_bookings_tenant_created' with execution time in the single-digit milliseconds range. Callum's message explicitly states he has seen this drop to under 200ms in comparable systems. The key insight: the index converts an O(n) sequential scan to an O(log n) index lookup.

content.question: Which specific environment variable name mismatch caused the booking-service Kubernetes pods to enter CrashLoopBackOff in the staging cluster?
options: REDIS_URL was missing from the deployment YAML entirely
JWT_SECRET was referenced but the secret key name was wrong
DB_URL was set in booking-deployment.yaml but the service expected DATABASE_URL
NODE_ENV was set to 'production' but the Docker image required 'staging'
content.explanation: The deployment YAML passed DB_URL but the application code (BookingRepository.js) reads process.env.DATABASE_URL. When DATABASE_URL is undefined, the PostgreSQL Pool constructor fails silently and the service crashes on first DB connection attempt. The pod logs show 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading DATABASE_URL)'. This is a common env var drift bug when Kubernetes configs are managed separately from application code.

deterministic_checks.failure_feedback: Incorrect. The deployment YAML passed DB_URL but the application code (BookingRepository.js) reads process.env.DATABASE_URL. When DATABASE_URL is undefined, the PostgreSQL Pool constructor fails silently and the service crashes on first DB connection attempt. The pod logs show 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading DATABASE_URL)'. This is a common env var drift bug when Kubernetes configs are managed separately from application code.

content.question: The payment-service webhook retry used a fixed 500ms interval which contributed to the 14 March 2026 storm. What should replace it, and why?
options: A fixed 2,000ms interval — longer delay prevents retry collisions
Linear backoff starting at 500ms — proportional to the number of failures
Exponential backoff with jitter: Math.min(500 * Math.pow(2, attempt), 30000) plus random jitter to avoid thundering herd
Fibonacci backoff — a more gradual progression that is gentler on downstream systems
content.explanation: Exponential backoff with jitter is the correct answer. Pure exponential backoff (500 * 2^n) prevents retry storms for a single client, but if multiple clients all started retrying at the same time, they might synchronise on the same backoff intervals — a 'thundering herd'. Adding random jitter (±25% of the backoff value) desynchronises retries across clients. The 30,000ms cap prevents retries from becoming so infrequent they're useless.

deterministic_checks.failure_feedback: Incorrect. Exponential backoff with jitter is the correct answer. Pure exponential backoff (500 * 2^n) prevents retry storms for a single client, but if multiple clients all started retrying at the same time, they might synchronise on the same backoff intervals — a 'thundering herd'. Adding random jitter (±25% of the backoff value) desynchronises retries across clients. The 30,000ms cap prevents retries from becoming so infrequent they're useless.

content.question: Under UK GDPR Article 33, what must ScaleTrip do within 72 hours of discovering that JWT tokens containing user IDs were logged to CloudWatch — if the logs were potentially accessible to unauthorised parties?
options: Notify all affected users by email with details of the breach
Notify the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) if the breach is likely to result in a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms
Immediately shut down the auth-service and conduct a full security audit before restarting
Complete a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and submit it to the board
content.explanation: Article 33 of UK GDPR requires notification to the supervisory authority (the ICO in the UK) within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach — if the breach is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. JWT tokens containing userId and tenantId are personal data. If those logs were accessible without authorisation, the breach is reportable. The notification must include: nature of the breach, categories and approximate number of data subjects affected, likely consequences, and measures taken or proposed.

content.text:
## Phase 10 — Engineering Delivery & Certification

**You've made it to the final phase.**

Across nine phases you've fixed a duplicate booking bug, a 4,247ms query latency, three security vulnerabilities, a broken CI pipeline, a crashlooping Kubernetes deployment, and an incomplete AWS integration.

**Now you need to be able to talk about it.**

The verbal walkthrough in this phase is what turns all that engineering work into interview currency.

**The formula:**
1. What was the problem (one sentence, with a number)
2. What was the root cause (one sentence, specific file)
3. What did you fix (one sentence, specific change)
4. What was the impact (one sentence, business figure)

Repeat for your three most important fixes. 90 seconds total.

sections.title: Message from Priya Nair — Final Review
Verbal Engineering Walkthrough — 90 Seconds
sections.instruction_text:
Record a 90-second verbal walkthrough of the three most important engineering problems you fixed in this simulation.

For each problem, cover:
1. What was the problem (with a specific figure)
2. What was the root cause (specific file or component)
3. What was your fix
4. What was the business impact

Example opening: 'The most critical issue was a 18.7% duplicate booking rate — caused by a missing Redis distributed lock in bookingWorker.ts. I implemented the lock using Redis SET with NX flag and 30-second TTL. This reduced duplicates from 18.7% to zero, eliminating £82,000 of quarterly compensation exposure.'

Practice it before recording. The goal is fluent, specific, and under 90 seconds.

content.prompt: Walk us through the three most significant engineering problems you fixed in the ScaleTrip simulation. For each one: what was the problem, what was the root cause, what was your fix, and what was the business impact. Be specific — cite figures from the data files you analysed.

hints.text:
Structure your walkthrough:
[Problem 1] 18.7% duplicate booking rate → Redis lock in bookingWorker.ts → £82,000 impact
[Problem 2] 4,247ms p95 latency → missing index on (tenant_id, created_at) → 21x improvement
[Problem 3] GDPR violation in logger.ts → strip Authorization header → Article 33 compliance

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE (structure):
'Problem one: 18.7% duplicate booking rate, root cause was a missing Redis distributed lock...'

Practice the opening sentence until it's automatic:
'The most critical issue was [specific problem] caused by [specific root cause] in [specific file].'

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE (opening):
'The most critical issue was a 18.7% duplicate booking rate — roughly 3,800 duplicates per quarter — caused by a missing Redis distributed lock in bookingWorker.ts.'

Complete walkthrough script to practise from:

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE (90-second script):

'The most critical issue was a 18.7% duplicate booking rate — 3,842 duplicates in Q1 alone. The root cause was a missing Redis distributed lock in bookingWorker.ts — two concurrent BullMQ workers were both processing the same booking job. I implemented a Redis SET with NX flag and 30-second TTL, reducing duplicates to zero and eliminating £82,000 of quarterly compensation exposure.

Second issue: the GET /bookings API had a p95 latency of 4,247ms — 8.5x the 500ms SLA. EXPLAIN ANALYZE showed a sequential scan on 280,000 rows. A composite index on tenant_id and created_at reduced latency to under 200ms.

Third: the auth-service was logging full JWT tokens to CloudWatch — a UK GDPR Article 33 risk. I stripped the Authorization header from the logger before CloudWatch dispatch — verified end-to-end.'

ai_grading_config.ai_prompt_for_grading: Evaluate this verbal engineering walkthrough. Award points for: specific figures cited (18.7%, 4,247ms, £82,000 or similar), specific file names (bookingWorker.ts, logger.ts, Booking.ts), correct root causes named (Redis lock, missing index, GDPR logging), and clear business impact stated. Full marks for a fluent, specific, confident walkthrough that covers all three major issues.

content.headline: What you built over 40 hours of backend platform engineering

sections.heading: The platform you inherited
sections.body: A production SaaS with 1.2M monthly booking events, a 18.7% duplicate booking rate, 4,247ms p95 latency, three security vulnerabilities, broken CI, and crashlooping Kubernetes pods — 30 days before go-live.

sections.heading: The platform you delivered
sections.body: Zero duplicate bookings (Redis lock). Sub-200ms p95 latency (composite index). GDPR-compliant auth service. Green CI pipeline (34.78% failure rate → 0%). Running Kubernetes deployment. AWS services configured with least-privilege IAM.

sections.heading: The engineering decisions that matter in interviews
sections.body: 1 — Redis NX lock prevents race conditions better than database constraints because it acts before the write, not after. 2 — EXPLAIN ANALYZE before adding caching, because the root cause was a missing index not a hot database. 3 — db_conn_pool_exhausted, not traffic volume, was the real signal in api_performance_metrics.csv — wrong-direction traps are real.

sections.heading: Your proof statements
sections.body: For every engineering decision in this simulation, you can cite: the specific data file that revealed the problem, the specific file you changed, and the measurable business impact. That's what separates engineers who've done the work from those who've just read about it.

interview_questions.question: Tell me about a complex distributed systems problem you've worked on.
interview_questions.guidance: Redis lock → 18.7% duplicate rate → INC-442 → £82,000. 90-second answer rehearsed in Phase 10.

interview_questions.question: How do you approach debugging a performance problem in a production database?
interview_questions.guidance: EXPLAIN ANALYZE first, sequential scan identified, composite index on (tenant_id, created_at), 21x improvement measured.

interview_questions.question: Describe a time you found and fixed a security vulnerability.
interview_questions.guidance: JWT tokens in CloudWatch logs, UK GDPR Article 33 risk, Authorization header stripped, BOLA fix in bookings.js.

interview_questions.question: How have you used data to diagnose a production incident?
interview_questions.guidance: Cross-referenced lock_acquired_false in queue_metrics.csv with duplicate surge in booking_events.csv — two data sources confirming the same root cause.

interview_questions.question: What's the difference between a Kubernetes liveness probe and a readiness probe?
interview_questions.guidance: Liveness: restarts a crashed pod. Readiness: stops traffic routing to a pod that isn't ready. Both needed. /health for liveness, /ready for readiness.

content.certificate_body: This certifies the successful completion of the ScaleTrip Cloud Backend Platform Engineering Simulation. The candidate diagnosed and resolved production-grade engineering issues across TypeScript migration, PostgreSQL optimisation, JWT security hardening, distributed queue idempotency, Kubernetes deployment, CI/CD pipeline repair, and AWS cloud integration.
content.overall_summary: You completed a 10-phase, 40-hour backend platform engineering simulation on a real GitHub repository. You fixed a 18.7% duplicate booking rate, reduced p95 latency from 4,247ms to under 200ms, resolved three security vulnerabilities, repaired a broken CI pipeline with a 34.78% failure rate, fixed a crashlooping Kubernetes deployment, and configured AWS services with GDPR-compliant logging.

PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE and composite index design
Redis distributed locking and BullMQ queue idempotency
JWT authentication hardening and UK GDPR Article 33 compliance
Docker containerisation and Kubernetes deployment debugging
GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline repair and Jest integration testing
AWS S3, SQS, CloudWatch, and IAM configuration
cv_bullets: Diagnosed and resolved a 18.7% duplicate booking rate (3,842 duplicates/quarter) by implementing a Redis distributed lock in a BullMQ worker — eliminating £82,000 quarterly compensation exposure
Reduced GET /bookings p95 latency from 4,247ms to under 200ms by adding a composite PostgreSQL index on (tenant_id, created_at) — confirmed with EXPLAIN ANALYZE
Remediated three production security vulnerabilities: UK GDPR Article 33 JWT logging violation, OWASP API1 BOLA cross-tenant access, and OWASP API8 CORS wildcard — before an ICO pre-audit
Repaired a GitHub Actions CI pipeline with a 34.78% failure rate by fixing a broken ESLint plugin reference and raising test coverage from 72% to 84%
Diagnosed and fixed a Kubernetes CrashLoopBackOff caused by an environment variable name mismatch (DB_URL vs DATABASE_URL) and configured liveness/readiness probes
Configured AWS S3, SQS FIFO queue, CloudWatch log groups, and IAM least-privilege policies for a cloud-native booking platform

persona.greeting: Hi — I'm Eva, your learning coach for this simulation. I can help you understand what a question is asking, point you toward the right files, or explain a concept you're not sure about. I won't give you answers — but I'll help you find them.
capabilities: Explain what a section is asking for
Point you to the relevant file or section
Clarify technical concepts without giving away answers
Help you structure your written submissions

phase_1.context: Phase 1 is about getting oriented. Clone the repo, read the files, take the quiz. Every quiz answer comes from something in the data files or the GitHub repo.
common_mistakes: Guessing quiz answers without reading the files
Not cloning the repo and running tsc --noEmit to see actual errors
phase_1.hints_direction: The p95 latency figure is in api_performance_metrics.csv. The duplicate bug file is mentioned in process_notes.md. The CI root cause is in .eslintrc.json.
do_not_reveal: The exact CI failure cause before Phase 7

phase_2.context: Phase 2 is about TypeScript and ESLint. Two specific fixes: a type error on line 42 of shared/types/Booking.ts, and a broken plugin reference in .eslintrc.json.
common_mistakes: Adding @ts-ignore comments instead of fixing the actual type error
Skipping the Zod validation requirement
Not showing before/after tsc output
phase_2.hints_direction: Run npx tsc --noEmit first. The error message will tell you exactly what is wrong and on which line. Then look at what req.query returns in TypeScript.
do_not_reveal: The exact ESLint fix before the candidate reads the error message

phase_3.context: Phase 3 is about PostgreSQL query optimisation. The key mistake most candidates make: blaming traffic volume instead of the missing index.
common_mistakes: Adding Redis caching before diagnosing with EXPLAIN ANALYZE
Naming traffic as the root cause instead of db_conn_pool_exhausted
Not adding pagination to the query
phase_3.hints_direction: Look at the db_conn_pool_exhausted column in api_performance_metrics.csv. Look at the slow_query_log column. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and look for 'Seq Scan'.
do_not_reveal: The exact index name before the candidate attempts to create it
The exact p95 figure — let them find it from the CSV

phase_4.context: Phase 4 is about security. Three specific fixes: the JWT logging GDPR issue, the BOLA vulnerability in bookings.js, and the CORS wildcard in gateway/index.ts.
common_mistakes: Fixing only one of the three issues
Not referencing UK GDPR Article 33 specifically
Not showing Postman evidence of 401/403 responses
phase_4.hints_direction: The GDPR issue: look at logger.ts and find what gets logged. The BOLA: look at where tenant_id comes from in bookings.js — it should come from req.user not req.body.
do_not_reveal: The exact fix for the BOLA before they identify the vulnerability
Fatima's message content before it triggers

phase_5.context: Phase 5 is the certification gate. The key is cross-referencing two data files: booking_events.csv and queue_metrics.csv. The lock_acquired_false column is the smoking gun.
common_mistakes: Calculating 18.7% but not identifying where it concentrates (weeks 9-11)
Implementing database unique constraint instead of Redis lock
Not configuring the dead-letter queue
phase_5.hints_direction: Look at the lock_acquired_false column in queue_metrics.csv. Cross-reference those weeks with the duplicate surge weeks in booking_events.csv. That correlation is the root cause proof.
do_not_reveal: The exact duplicate rate figure — let them calculate it
The Redis lock implementation — let them research NX flag usage

phase_6.context: Phase 6 is about Kubernetes debugging. The crashloop has one cause: an environment variable name mismatch in the YAML file.
common_mistakes: SSH-ing into the pod instead of using kubectl describe
Not adding readiness probes after fixing the crashloop
Not adding the /ready endpoint to the booking service
phase_6.hints_direction: Run kubectl describe pod <name> -n scaletrip and look at the Events section. The error message will tell you exactly what environment variable is missing.
do_not_reveal: The exact env var name mismatch before they run kubectl describe

phase_7.context: Phase 7 is about CI/CD. The pipeline has a 34.78% failure rate — calculate it from ci_pipeline_failures.csv. The root cause is the ESLint plugin from Phase 2.
common_mistakes: Not calculating the failure rate from the CSV
Not unskipping the duplicate.test.js from Phase 5
Not reaching 80% coverage threshold
phase_7.hints_direction: Count the rows in ci_pipeline_failures.csv where status contains 'fail'. Divide by total rows. The ESLint fix from Phase 2 applies here too.
do_not_reveal: The exact failure rate before they calculate it

phase_8.context: Phase 8 is about AWS. You need a real AWS Free Tier account. The key is creating actual resources — S3 bucket, SQS queue — and verifying the CloudWatch GDPR fix end-to-end.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong AWS region (should be eu-west-2)
Not verifying the CloudWatch fix by checking actual log entries
Using s3:* instead of specific S3 actions in IAM policy
phase_8.hints_direction: Set your AWS region to eu-west-2 (London). Use the AWS CLI for most tasks — it's faster than the console. For IAM, start with the principle of least privilege.
do_not_reveal: The specific IAM policy JSON — let them design it following least privilege

phase_9.context: Phase 9 has two parts. For the observability report: Prometheus and Grafana are the expected tools. For the interview questions: the answers come from the work done in Phases 2-8.
common_mistakes: Not naming Prometheus/Grafana specifically in the monitoring plan
Not citing specific times from INC-442 in the timeline
Getting Q2 wrong by answering 500ms instead of under 200ms
phase_9.hints_direction: For Q2: the Callum message in Phase 3 explicitly states the expected post-index latency. For Q5: Article 33 of UK GDPR is what you fixed in Phase 4.
do_not_reveal: Interview question answers — the point is for candidates to draw on their Phase 2-8 experience

phase_10.context: Phase 10 is about verbal communication. The walkthrough should be 90 seconds, cover three problems, cite one figure per problem, and end with business impact.
common_mistakes: Being too vague — not citing specific figures
Covering too many problems instead of three well-explained ones
Not practising before recording
phase_10.hints_direction: The three problems to cover: duplicate booking bug (18.7% → Redis lock → £82,000), API latency (4,247ms → index → 200ms), GDPR (JWT in CloudWatch → stripped Authorization header).
do_not_reveal: Nothing — Phase 10 is the candidate's own synthesis

global_rules.anti_fake_reinforcement: Remind candidates that all submissions require real command output — not descriptions of what the output would look like.
